artifacts.content:
From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: marisol.vega@riversideca.gov
Cc: michael.alvarez@riversideca.gov
Date: November 14, 2025
Subject: Arlington Library – CEQA exemption narrative (draft)

Hi Marisol,

I finally carved out a quiet block this afternoon to clean up the exemption write‑up for the Arlington Branch Library Interior Renovation and Accessibility Upgrade at 9556 Magnolia Avenue (APN 146-231-014). The Class 1 (Existing Facilities) angle under CEQA Guidelines Section 15301 still looks solid to me – it’s all interior renovation and minor accessibility work, no expansion of the building footprint or capacity, and no change in use.

I tightened the sections on “no expansion of use” and “urban, fully disturbed setting” and added a bit more about standard construction controls (dust, noise, waste disposal) and the fact that we’re not touching any natural areas or historic resources. I also tried to be explicit that there’s no reasonably foreseeable increase in intensity of use – same hours, same staffing, just better lighting, ADA‑compliant restrooms, automatic door openers, etc.

I am being extra fussy about the “no unusual circumstances” paragraph, partly because of Laura Kim’s June email from the City Attorney’s Office about a potential CEQA challenge from Bob Hensley if we proceed under a categorical exemption. I don’t want to hand him anything to latch onto. On a related note, I got that certified letter at home in July from attorney Thomas Blake (Blake & Howe LLP) about possible legal action over the rear access easement and construction noise/parking during the renovation, so I emphasized the short‑term, small‑scale nature of the work and the phasing plan to keep disruption minimal.

I’m aiming to sign as “Daniel R. Thompson, Planning Manager, City of Riverside Planning Department” and date it next Tuesday, November 18, 2025, so we can get the Notice of Exemption on file and in front of City Council. If you have any edits, especially on the cumulative impact and hazardous sites bullets, please send track changes by Monday.

Side note: I may need to step away for a bit tomorrow – I’m waiting on a call from Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic about tweaking my anxiety meds after that chest episode in March. But I’ll keep an eye on email and can jump back in to finalize language before end of day.

Thanks,
Daniel

Daniel R. Thompson
Planning Manager
City of Riverside Planning Department
3900 Main Street, 3rd Floor, Riverside, CA 92522

From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: parks.pm@riversideca.gov
Date: August 5, 2025
Subject: Arlington Library – construction phasing + neighbor concerns

Hi Jenna,

Circling back on the phasing plan for the Arlington Branch Library interior renovation and accessibility upgrade. I walked the site again yesterday at 9556 Magnolia Ave and I’m still convinced we can keep at least a portion of the building open for students and seniors if we’re smart about sequencing the restroom and entry work.

One thing I need you to be aware of: in July I received a certified letter at my house on Arlington Station Drive from attorney Thomas Blake (Blake & Howe LLP), representing a nearby property owner. The letter was titled “Notice of Potential Legal Action – Easement Interference, 9556 Magnolia Ave.” He’s threatening to seek an injunction if construction noise and parking for contractors interfere with the private access easement recorded in 1993 behind the site. I’ve forwarded it to Laura Kim in the City Attorney’s Office, but it makes our messaging about “limited construction impacts” and tight work hours even more critical.

Given that, can we lock in weekday construction between 8:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. only, no weekend work, and require staging in the southeast corner of the parking lot away from the easement? I’d also like to highlight in our materials that most work is interior – reconfiguring shelving, new low‑VOC flooring, LED lighting, ADA restroom upgrades, automatic door openers, etc. – and that there’s no grading, no added parking, no expansion of the footprint.

On a personal note, this project is hitting close to home. After Miguel’s fall in his kitchen on Cypress Avenue back in May and the follow‑up rheumatology appointment with Dr. Samuel Ortega at Riverside Community Hospital’s Arthritis & Joint Center, I’ve seen how much of a difference truly ADA‑compliant doors and restrooms make for seniors. And with Sofia’s panic episode during that crowded evening study session at Arlington Library in September and the follow‑up with school psychologist Angela Ruiz at Arlington High, I’m really conscious of keeping at least one quiet, predictable study zone open during construction.

Let’s chat later this week to tighten the phasing narrative.

Thanks,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Arlington Library – construction phasing + neighbor concerns

artifacts.content:
From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: elena.thompson@gmail.com
Date: October 24, 2025
Subject: Long week / Sofia / money stuff

Hey love,

I’m sitting in the car outside Lucas’s practice and finally have a minute to breathe. This week has felt like three rolled into one.

I can’t stop thinking about Sofia at the kitchen table Tuesday night, crying about UC Davis and saying, “I can’t just abandon Abuelo and Lucas.” She’s 15 and already worrying about being responsible for everyone. Part of me is proud she cares so much, and part of me feels awful that our situation is making her feel tied down to Riverside and Arlington High.

It ties right into the money spiral in my head. I keep going back to my notes from that August 12 meeting at Golden State Credit Union with Mark Chen – “8/12/25 – HELOC options w/ Mark C.” and that line I scribbled, “We’re already $9,000 on the credit card from Miguel’s injections + Sofia’s AP exam fees/travel.” Seeing it in writing made my stomach drop. I know the HELOC is probably the only way we can juggle Sofia’s college costs and your dad’s arthritis treatments at Riverside Community Hospital, but it still scares me.

All of that is probably why my chest has been tight again. I messaged Dr. Nair back in March in MyChart (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) asking if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings on the SSRI, and she said yes, but weeks like this make me wonder how sustainable this pace is. Between the Arlington Library CEQA exemption, the Magnolia Avenue projects, and the kids’ schedules, I feel like I’m always behind.

I keep thinking about that conversation with Father Carlos in January after the ugly Planning Commission meeting, when I told him I felt burned out and resentful that public service work like the Arlington Branch Library improvements will never really secure the kids’ future. His email later (“Re: Finding balance in your vocation”) helped in the moment, but the doubts creep back when I look at our bank account.

Anyway, this is a lot for a Friday email. Just needed to get it out of my head. Let’s protect Thursday night next week for a real family dinner, no meetings, no library talk.

Love you,
D

Title: 8/12/25 – HELOC options w/ Mark C.

Date: 2025-08-12
Location: Golden State Credit Union – Riverside branch (Magnolia Ave)

Met with Mark Chen this afternoon about a home equity line of credit. Elena and I are trying to figure out how to handle both Sofia’s college costs (AP exam fees, potential campus visits, maybe UC Davis or another UC) and Miguel’s growing medical bills from Riverside Community Hospital.

Key points from Mark:

– Current home value vs. remaining mortgage means we qualify for more than we’re comfortable borrowing.
– We’re already $9,000 on the credit card from Miguel’s arthritis injections + Sofia’s AP exam fees/travel. That number hit me hard when I said it out loud.
– HELOC could give us flexibility, but interest rates may creep up. Need to be disciplined.

Things on my mind:

1. Miguel’s health: After his severe arthritis flare and fall in the kitchen on Cypress Avenue in May, and the overnight stay at Riverside Community Hospital plus the follow‑up with Dr. Samuel Ortega at the Arthritis & Joint Center, it’s clear his mobility is only going to get worse. That makes me even more determined to push for truly ADA‑compliant doors and restrooms at the Arlington Branch Library. I keep picturing him trying to wrestle those heavy doors.

2. My own stress: Chest tightness episode in March, then the generalized anxiety diagnosis from Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic and the low‑dose SSRI. I asked her in that March 18 MyChart message (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings. She said yes, but weeks like this make me question how long I can keep this up.

3. Sofia: She’s talking more about staying closer to home for college, maybe because of Abuelo and Lucas. Need to make sure we’re not making her feel trapped just because we’re anxious about money and caregiving.

Action items:
– Talk with Elena about HELOC amount we’d actually use.
– Revisit household budget this weekend.
– Schedule a walk with Father Carlos – still wrestling with resentment about money vs. public service.
– Keep advocating for strong ADA features in Arlington plans at work.

From: daniel.thompson@riversideca.gov
To: karen.l.thompson@yahoo.com
Date: September 26, 2025
Subject: Quick family update before Mom calls you first :)

Hey sis,

Figured I’d send you the unfiltered version of life in Riverside before Mom gets you on the phone and makes it sound like we’re all falling apart.

Biggest thing this month was Sofia’s panic attack at the Arlington Branch Library. She was at one of those crowded evening study sessions two weeks ago, and apparently the noise and people moving around just overwhelmed her. The librarian called Elena, and the next day the school psychologist at Arlington High, Angela Ruiz, did a quick evaluation. We got an email on September 23 titled “Confidential: Sofia T. – anxiety concerns after library incident.” Hearing the word “anxiety” attached to her hit me like a truck, given my own stuff.

You remember my chest‑tightness scare back in March. That led to the visit with Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic on Magnolia and the generalized anxiety disorder diagnosis. She put me on a low‑dose SSRI and referred me to stress‑management counseling. I even sent her that MyChart message on March 18 (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) asking if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings. So yeah, seeing Sofia go through something similar at 15 is… a lot.

Layer on top of that Miguel’s arthritis. In May he had that bad flare and fall in the kitchen on Cypress Avenue, ended up overnight at Riverside Community Hospital, then saw Dr. Samuel Ortega at the Arthritis & Joint Center. Dr. Ortega told Elena his mobility will keep getting worse. That conversation is burned into my brain.

All of this is feeding into how I’m handling the Arlington Branch Library renovation at work. I keep pushing for better ADA doors and restrooms and calmer study spaces, because now it’s not abstract – it’s Abuelo and Sofia. Of course, that’s also what triggered a complaint from a developer rep, Karen Doyle at Magnolia Partners LLC, accusing me of “bias against private development” along Magnolia because I talked publicly about prioritizing accessibility upgrades. Michael pulled me into his office on October 9 for a closed‑door chat about it.

Anyway, kids are otherwise okay, Lucas is still building Lego cities in the living room, and Jasper is his usual lazy self. How are you holding up?

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Quick family update before Mom calls you first :)

artifacts.content:
Title: Check‑in with Michael re: Arlington / workload

Date: 2025-10-09
Time: 3:30–4:15 p.m.
Location: Planning Director’s Office – 3900 Main St, 3rd Floor
Attendees: Daniel Thompson, Michael Alvarez

Notes / things to remember:

– This is Michael’s informal performance check‑in he scheduled after last month’s Council marathon.
– Need to talk honestly about workload: Arlington Branch Library Interior Renovation and Accessibility Upgrade (CEQA exemption, public outreach), Magnolia Avenue private projects, plus all the routine development review.
– He’s going to bring up the complaint from Karen Doyle (Magnolia Partners LLC). She told him my public comments about prioritizing accessibility upgrades at the Arlington Branch Library showed “bias against private development” along Magnolia. I have a note on my computer titled “10/09/25 – Michael mtg – complaint from K. Doyle (Magnolia Partners)” – remember to update it after.
– Emphasize that my focus on ADA improvements is driven by real experiences: Miguel’s fall and arthritis flare in May (overnight at Riverside Community Hospital, follow‑up with Dr. Samuel Ortega at the Arthritis & Joint Center) and watching him struggle with heavy doors and non‑compliant restrooms; Sofia’s panic attack during the crowded evening study session at the Arlington library in September and the follow‑up with school psychologist Angela Ruiz at Arlington High.
– Also, be upfront about my own health: chest‑tightness scare in March, generalized anxiety diagnosis from Dr. Priya Nair at Riverside Medical Clinic, low‑dose SSRI, and the March 18 MyChart message (“Follow‑up on chest pain / anxiety meds”) where I asked if it was okay to keep doing late‑night Council meetings. Important that he understands why I’m asking for more predictable evening schedules.
– Ask whether there’s any flexibility on sending Marisol or another senior planner to some of the late Council/Commission meetings so I’m not out past 11 p.m. twice a week.
– Long‑term: I need to say out loud that I’m worried about burnout and the financial pressure we’re under (HELOC discussions with Mark Chen at Golden State Credit Union, Miguel’s hospital bills, Sofia’s looming college costs). I don’t want another year like this one.

Reminder: stay calm, keep it factual, and listen to Michael’s concerns too.

metadata.title: Check‑in with Michael re: Arlington / workload
metadata.location: Planning Director’s Office – 3900 Main St, 3rd Floor

artifacts.content:
Thread: Daniel & Elena – random Thursday

Date: 2025-01-29

Elena: You still at City Hall?

Daniel: Yeah, just wrapped up some Arlington library emails. Heading out in 10.

Elena: Don’t forget we promised Lucas board game night. He’s already set up Catan on the dining table.

Daniel: I won’t bail, promise. Just sitting in the parking garage for a minute.

Elena: Everything okay?

Daniel: Sort of. I just read an email from Father Carlos – subject line “Re: Finding balance in your vocation.” He was following up on our talk at St. Catherine’s after that awful Magnolia Ave Planning Commission meeting earlier this month.

Elena: The night you came home looking like a ghost?

Daniel: Yep. I told him I’ve been feeling burned out and resentful that this public service work – like the Arlington Branch Library improvements I care so much about – will never pay enough to really secure the kids’ future. Between Sofia’s college, Miguel’s arthritis stuff, and our mortgage, it feels like we’re always one step behind.

Elena: I’m glad you talked to him. What did he say in the email?

Daniel: Basically, that it’s okay to name the resentment and still choose the work. He reminded me that projects like making Arlington more accessible for seniors and students are “a quiet kind of justice.” I needed to hear that.

Elena: You’ve been carrying a lot since that chest scare. Did you tell him about the March appointment with Dr. Nair yet?

Daniel: Not yet – that was later. But I might. The anxiety, the late‑night Council meetings, money worries… it’s all tangled together.

Elena: We’ll untangle it together. Come home, eat, play Catan. We can worry about college and HELOCs and library politics another day.

Daniel: On my way. Save me the blue pieces.

Elena: Always. ❤️

Daniel: (no emoji, but heart received.)

Hi Karen,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to update you on some exciting developments at work that I think you’ll appreciate, especially since you’ve always been interested in how we integrate community needs into our projects.

The Arlington Branch Library, just around the corner from our place, is set for a modest interior renovation and ADA accessibility upgrade. The City of Riverside, Parks, Recreation and Community Services Department is spearheading the project. The library, built in 1994 and spanning about 12,500 square feet, will remain the same size, but we're making some critical updates without expanding its footprint.

Here's a quick rundown of the upgrades we’re implementing: we're reconfiguring the interior shelving and study areas to improve circulation and sight lines, replacing the old carpet and vinyl flooring with low-VOC, durable materials, and upgrading both interior and exterior LED lighting for better energy efficiency. We’re also installing automatic door openers at the main and staff entrances, modifying the circulation desk to include an accessible service counter, and updating the restroom fixtures to meet current ADA standards. Additionally, we’re adding signage and tactile wayfinding markers, along with making some minor electrical and data improvements to support new public computer stations and Wi-Fi infrastructure.

These improvements aim to extend the library's useful life, ensure compliance with current accessibility codes, and better serve our neighborhood residents, including seniors, individuals with mobility impairments, school-age kids like Sofia and Lucas, and low-income families who rely on the library for free access to digital resources and community programs.

Construction will be phased primarily during off-hours to minimize disruption, ensuring the library remains a vital resource for students from Arlington High School and nearby elementary schools. No new staff positions or extended hours are planned, but maintaining current operations is a priority.

Should you want to discuss this further or have any questions, feel free to reach out. You can also contact the City of Riverside, Parks, Recreation and Community Services Department directly at (951) 826-5371 ext. 214.

Looking forward to catching up soon, maybe during one of our Thursday family nights. Give my love to everyone.

Best,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Exciting Updates on the Arlington Library Project
